title: Supabase Service Role JWT secrets should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: **CRITICAL SECURITY ALERT**: Supabase SERVICE_ROLE JWT token detected in source code. SERVICE_ROLE tokens are ADMINISTRATIVE SUPERUSER credentials providing unrestricted access to your entire Supabase project with complete bypass of all security policies. These tokens grant FULL ADMINISTRATIVE PRIVILEGES including: UNRESTRICTED database access (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP, ALTER) on ALL PostgreSQL tables bypassing Row Level Security (RLS) policies completely, administrative control over database schema (CREATE/DROP tables, ALTER schemas, modify constraints), real-time subscriptions to ALL database changes across entire project, complete authentication system control (create/modify/delete user accounts, change authentication settings, access user credentials and PII), FULL storage bucket access (read/write/delete ALL files, modify bucket policies, access private files), edge functions with elevated privileges (access to service_role secrets, unrestricted API calls), database functions and stored procedures with superuser rights, direct PostgreSQL access with admin credentials, project settings modification (change billing, security settings, API keys), backup and restore operations, point-in-time recovery controls, and complete project infrastructure manipulation. SERVICE_ROLE tokens are equivalent to PostgreSQL superuser credentials combined with full administrative dashboard access - they are THE MOST SENSITIVE SECRETS in your Supabase project. Exposing SERVICE_ROLE tokens allows attackers to: IMMEDIATELY access and exfiltrate ALL data from every table regardless of RLS policies (complete database dump including PII, financial records, credentials, business secrets), permanently delete or corrupt critical data (DROP tables, TRUNCATE data, modify schemas destroying application functionality), create administrative backdoor accounts with permanent access (insert into auth.users with elevated privileges, create service accounts, establish persistent access mechanisms), upload malicious files or malware to ALL storage buckets (public and private) consuming unlimited quota, execute arbitrary SQL commands including system commands if PostgreSQL extensions enabled (potential server compromise, data exfiltration to external systems, ransomware deployment), modify RLS policies removing all security controls (disable security for other users, create policy backdoors, expose sensitive data), enumerate complete database architecture (extract full schema, discover business logic, identify security weaknesses, map data relationships), hijack user authentication flows and sessions (impersonate any user, steal session tokens, create fake authentication flows), modify application security settings (disable MFA, change password policies, weaken security controls), access ALL environment variables and secrets stored in edge functions (API keys for third-party services, database credentials, encryption keys), manipulate billing and project settings (upgrade to expensive tiers, change payment methods, lock legitimate administrators out), delete critical backups and recovery points (preventing disaster recovery, enabling ransomware attacks), abuse API rate limits and compute resources to cause complete service disruption (DDoS-style attacks, resource exhaustion, service degradation affecting all users), inject malicious edge functions or triggers (establish persistence, monitor all application activity, steal credentials), access audit logs and security settings to cover tracks (delete evidence, manipulate monitoring, evade detection), pivot to connected systems and services using stored third-party credentials (compromise AWS/GCP/Azure resources, access payment gateways, infiltrate SaaS integrations), and potentially gain shell access to underlying infrastructure if PostgreSQL extensions allow system commands. The severity is ALWAYS CRITICAL and MAXIMUM - there is NO scenario where SERVICE_ROLE key exposure is acceptable. Unlike ANON keys which are somewhat protected by RLS policies, SERVICE_ROLE keys provide COMPLETE UNRESTRICTED ACCESS equivalent to having root/admin credentials for your entire backend infrastructure. A compromised SERVICE_ROLE key means TOTAL COMPROMISE of your Supabase project with potential for: complete data breach of ALL customer data and PII violating GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2, and other compliance frameworks; permanent data loss through malicious deletion or corruption; ransomware attacks encrypting or holding your database hostage; reputational destruction from data breach disclosure; regulatory fines potentially millions of dollars for compliance violations; legal liability from exposed customer data including class action lawsuits; business continuity failure from service disruption; financial fraud through manipulated user accounts or payment data; intellectual property theft of proprietary business logic and data; competitive intelligence loss revealing strategic information; customer trust erosion causing business failure; incident response costs including forensics, legal fees, notification expenses; credit monitoring obligations for affected customers; insurance premium increases or policy cancellations; stock price impact for public companies; mandatory breach notifications to regulatory bodies; potential criminal liability for executives in regulated industries; and complete loss of customer confidence requiring possible business shutdown. FINANCIAL IMPACT is potentially catastrophic: unlimited compute charges from malicious operations, unlimited storage consumption from data duplication or malicious uploads, bandwidth costs from data exfiltration (entire database downloads), forced plan upgrades to maximum tier, emergency security services and incident response (typically $200K+ for Fortune 500 companies), legal and regulatory fines (GDPR fines up to 4% of global revenue or €20M whichever is greater), customer compensation for data breach, credit monitoring services for affected users, cyber insurance deductibles and uncovered losses, business interruption losses during recovery, reputational damage affecting sales and customer acquisition, and potential company failure for startups without capital reserves. IMMEDIATE EMERGENCY ACTIONS REQUIRED - Execute within 1 hour: 1) **CRITICAL FIRST STEP**: Revoke compromised SERVICE_ROLE token IMMEDIATELY in Supabase dashboard (Project Settings → API → Service Role Key → Regenerate) - this is HIGHEST PRIORITY, do this BEFORE any other action, every second counts; 2) Generate new SERVICE_ROLE key with proper segregation and IMMEDIATELY rotate it into secure backend services only (use secrets managers, NEVER commit to code); 3) Initiate FULL security incident response: assemble incident response team, document exposure timeline, preserve evidence for forensics, prepare for breach notification obligations, contact legal counsel, prepare regulatory notifications if PII exposed, activate cyber insurance; 4) EMERGENCY database audit of exposure window: review ALL Supabase logs for unauthorized access (query logs, authentication logs, API access logs), check '_supabase_admin' schema for suspicious modifications, audit auth.users table for fake accounts or privilege escalations, inspect auth.audit_log_entries for security events, review storage buckets for malicious uploads or data exfiltration, audit edge function invocations for malicious code execution, check for new database objects (tables, functions, triggers, schemas), review RLS policy modifications, examine backup integrity and verify no backup deletions, check for SQL injection attempts or suspicious queries, audit database schema changes, review network connection logs for unusual IP addresses; 5) CRITICAL data protection LOCKDOWN: enable database read-only mode if compromise detected (prevents further damage), snapshot current database state for forensics, verify backup integrity and create emergency backup, review and immediately tighten ALL RLS policies (assume attacker may have created backdoors), audit ALL table permissions, enable database activity monitoring with alerting, implement emergency IP allowlisting if possible (block all except known good IPs), rotate ALL other credentials (database passwords, other API keys, storage credentials, third-party service tokens); 6) User protection actions: force password reset for ALL users if auth system accessed, invalidate ALL user sessions immediately (prevents hijacked sessions), enable MFA requirement for all accounts, review user table for unauthorized modifications or fake accounts, audit authentication settings for policy weakening, check for backdoor accounts with admin privileges, notify users of potential compromise if PII accessed (legal requirement), prepare breach notification letters; 7) Application emergency response: replace compromised SERVICE_ROLE keys in ALL backend services (identify every location storing this key), verify no SERVICE_ROLE keys exist in frontend code, mobile apps, or public repositories, audit ALL applications using Supabase for compromise, implement emergency authentication controls, enable additional security logging, deploy application updates to production immediately, conduct code review of all Supabase integration points, check for injected malicious code in edge functions; 8) Implement STRICT secrets management immediately: store new SERVICE_ROLE keys EXCLUSIVELY in enterprise secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault Enterprise, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, CyberArk) with encryption at rest and in transit, implement just-in-time secrets access with short-lived credentials (rotate every 24 hours if possible), enforce principle of least privilege (create separate service accounts with limited permissions instead of using SERVICE_ROLE when possible), maintain separate SERVICE_ROLE keys per environment with strict segregation (production keys NEVER touch development), implement secrets rotation automation with zero-downtime deployment, enable comprehensive audit logging of all secrets access, configure alerting for unexpected secrets access, document complete inventory of all systems with SERVICE_ROLE access, implement approval workflows for SERVICE_ROLE key access, use Kubernetes secrets with RBAC, namespace isolation, and encryption at rest; 9) IMMEDIATE security hardening LOCKDOWN: enable MFA on ALL Supabase dashboard accounts, implement IP allowlisting on Supabase project (restrict to corporate VPN, known data centers), configure strictest rate limiting on all API endpoints, enable database firewall rules, activate Supabase audit logging at maximum verbosity, integrate Supabase logs with SIEM (Splunk, ELK, Sentinel) for correlation, implement real-time anomaly detection on database activity, enable storage bucket access logs with monitoring, configure webhook alerts for ALL administrative operations, implement network segmentation isolating Supabase access, deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) protecting API endpoints, enable DDoS protection, configure database connection limits; 10) RLS policy EMERGENCY review (assume compromise): audit EVERY table for RLS enablement status, review ALL RLS policies for backdoors or weakening (attacker may have modified), test policies under various authentication scenarios, implement RLS testing in CI/CD with security gates, enable RLS by default on ALL tables with explicit deny policies, implement emergency policy templates for critical tables, audit policy modification history, document security model and policy intent, enable RLS policy versioning and review, implement automated RLS compliance checking, consider implementing additional application-layer authorization as defense-in-depth; 11) Enhanced monitoring and detection deployment: set up real-time alerts for: ANY database schema changes, ANY RLS policy modifications, ANY new user account creation, ANY authentication setting changes, ANY service_role API usage (should be rare), ANY database admin operations, geographic anomalies in API requests, failed authentication attempts exceeding threshold, API usage exceeding baseline, storage operations outside normal patterns, edge function deployments or modifications, backup deletion attempts, project settings changes; implement Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) integration with correlation rules, deploy User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA), establish baseline metrics for anomaly detection, implement synthetic monitoring detecting service abuse, configure distributed tracing for attack path analysis; 12) Infrastructure security lockdown: review ALL database connection security and certificates, enforce TLS 1.3 for all Supabase connections, audit network policies and firewall rules across infrastructure, verify backup encryption and test restore procedures, enable database point-in-time recovery with protected backups, implement infrastructure as code for Supabase configuration (version controlled, reviewed), maintain separate Supabase projects for different security zones (production isolated completely), implement zero trust network architecture, deploy bastion hosts for administrative access, enable VPC peering or PrivateLink for Supabase connections; 13) Incident response and forensics: document complete incident timeline with evidence preservation, conduct digital forensics on all affected systems, preserve all logs before retention periods expire, conduct root cause analysis identifying how token was exposed, perform damage assessment quantifying data accessed or modified, identify all systems potentially compromised, review incident response effectiveness, prepare detailed incident report for leadership, document lessons learned, update incident response playbooks, conduct tabletop exercises for future incidents, prepare breach notification to regulatory bodies (within 72 hours for GDPR), notify affected customers as required by law, prepare communication strategy for media inquiries, engage external security firm for forensic investigation if needed, preserve evidence for potential law enforcement involvement; 14) Compliance and regulatory response: immediately notify Data Protection Officer (DPO), review regulatory obligations (GDPR 72-hour notification, state breach laws), prepare breach notifications with legal review, document compliance with security frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001), notify cyber insurance carrier, conduct compliance impact assessment, prepare for regulatory audits or investigations, update risk register and risk treatment plans, conduct board-level briefing on incident, prepare SEC disclosures if public company, review contractual obligations to customers, prepare customer notification and FAQ, document remediation actions for regulators, establish ongoing monitoring and reporting; 15) Long-term organizational response ($500K+ investment for enterprise): hire external security firm for comprehensive security audit (plan $100K+), conduct penetration testing of entire Supabase implementation ($50K+), implement code scanning automation (GitHub Advanced Security, Snyk, Checkmarx), deploy secrets scanning across all repositories with blocking pre-commit hooks (git-secrets, truffleHog, detect-secrets), implement mandatory security training for all engineers (annual requirement), establish Security Champions program with Supabase specialists, create and enforce Supabase security standards and policies, implement mandatory security review for ALL Supabase code (require two approvals), establish secrets rotation automation with runbooks, conduct quarterly architecture security reviews, deploy continuous secrets scanning in CI/CD pipelines with deployment blocking, implement Supabase usage inventory and governance, conduct annual third-party security assessments, maintain 24/7 security operations center (SOC) monitoring, implement bug bounty program including Supabase security, establish vendor security management for Supabase, create disaster recovery and business continuity plans specific to Supabase compromise scenarios. **ABSOLUTE PROHIBITIONS for SERVICE_ROLE keys**: NEVER EVER use SERVICE_ROLE keys in: frontend/client-side code (web, mobile, desktop applications), public Git repositories or any version control, environment variable files (.env) committed to repositories, CI/CD configuration files, documentation or wiki pages, screenshots or screen recordings, configuration files in containers or Docker images, Kubernetes ConfigMaps (must use Secrets with encryption), infrastructure as code templates (Terraform/CloudFormation - use secrets managers), mobile application code (impossible to secure in apps), JavaScript/browser code (inspectable by users), embed in URLs or query parameters, email or Slack messages, shared notes or password managers without encryption, unencrypted backups or exports, log files or monitoring systems, error messages or stack traces, API responses or client-facing interfaces, third-party services without review, development or test environments (use separate keys), local developer machines (use scoped development keys), shared development servers, or ANY location accessible to non-security-cleared personnel. **REQUIRED SECURITY ARCHITECTURE**: SERVICE_ROLE keys should ONLY exist in: production backend servers with strict access controls, enterprise secrets management systems (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.), secure CI/CD secrets with audit logging, encrypted production configuration deployed at runtime, highly restricted administrative access requiring MFA and approval, air-gapped security systems, Hardware Security Modules (HSM) for highest security environments. Treat SERVICE_ROLE keys with HIGHER security than: production database passwords, AWS root credentials, SSL/TLS private keys, code signing certificates, and encryption master keys - they are equivalent to complete application takeover credentials. Any SERVICE_ROLE key exposure is reportable security incident requiring: immediate executive notification, full incident response activation, potential regulatory notification, customer notification if PII accessed, comprehensive forensic investigation, security audit by external firm, and documentation for compliance audits. For enterprise organizations: implement break-glass procedures for SERVICE_ROLE access, require dual control for SERVICE_ROLE operations, implement privileged access management (PAM), enable session recording for admin operations, conduct quarterly SERVICE_ROLE key rotation, maintain complete audit logs for 2+ years, implement anomaly detection on SERVICE_ROLE usage, and establish security metrics dashboards. **REFERENCES AND EMERGENCY RESOURCES**: Supabase Security Documentation: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/platform/security, Supabase Management API for programmatic key rotation: https://supabase.com/docs/reference/api, Supabase Database Webhooks for monitoring: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/webhooks, Supabase Audit Logs: contact support for enterprise audit log access, Emergency Supabase Support: support@supabase.io (mark URGENT SECURITY INCIDENT), Incident Response Framework: NIST SP 800-61r2, Breach Notification Requirements: GDPR Article 33, CCPA §1798.82, HIPAA §164.410, Secrets Management Best Practices: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet, Cloud Security Alliance Secrets Management Guidelines. This is a CRITICAL SEVERITY finding requiring IMMEDIATE action - every hour of exposure increases compromise severity exponentially. Activate incident response immediately and treat this as highest priority security emergency requiring all-hands response.
